sentences.text: If I didn't see 80 of them, I didn't see one.
It was far more than 80 mass graves.
There were-- every every town we went through, there was a mass grave.
There was a mass grave.
And some of them, when the, when the weather started to get warmer, they just buried them.
They didn't even cover them properly.
And they started to expose.
Some of them started to expose.
And of course, Jewish were-- Jewish, Jews would automatically, and the rest, there were Russian people.
There was no word to us.
There was no word.
The army did not disclose anything to us.
They didn't send any, any flyers.
I don't know why it was done like that.
I think it would have been much better if you were all mature.
We already were battle, you know, trained in everything else and seasoned soldiers.
And I think it would have been much better.
It was a more shock to enter like that if you would have known that that's what really was going on.
Well, we were horrified, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it is hard to describe for one reason.
We had our future ahead of us, and we simply could not lose our cool.
Even so we were in the back, but mentally we could not allow ourselves to be in the position not to think only about the dead people.
We had to think about the people alive.
We had to think about the people which we left there in Poland because that's the reason we're going there.
So we simply couldn't allow ourselves to do it.
Yes, it was battle iron.
There were silent cries, cries, you know, and something like that because you were always afraid.
All these people, all these Gentile soldiers which were with me, their families were not in Russia.
Some of them were not in Russia.
Because they wouldn't know even where they were.
Maybe they were, and they didn't know where they were because they were less than the-- and the western territory which was occupied by the Russians.
But most of those guys were in POW camp.
So they knew that they left their families there.
So you simply could not allow yourself to do it.
It was hard.
It was hard, but nevertheless, we dealt with it like that.
I mean, I'm talking now 50 years later, and of course, you know, it's easy to express myself now.
But before I know that, the only thing what we did really, the Jews were praying in their own religion.
It was not forbidden.
And the Gentiles were every day, you know, we were a religious group.
Believe it or not, in Russia we were.
We didn't care about the KBG.
They were kneeling.
They were praying.
We were priest-- they had priests.
We had only-- we had one rabbi.
Unfortunately, he died.
But nevertheless, in the Jewish religion, you don't need a rabbi to pray.
You pray by yourself.
And I had my praying book with me.
I had my praying book with me.
And there was no shawls because in orthodox Poland, a Jewish kid, a Jewish boy after a bar mitzvah, he did not wear any shawls.
The only thing what you wear is the tefillin.
And that object I had with me.
And that object was inspected by one of the KBG people in Lwow before they took me out.
And I was a kid, you know, then I was what? 15, 15 and a half or something.
Or 16 years old.
And I explained it what it is.
I said, I'm using it for my daily prayer.
There's nothing in there.
It's not a gun.
It's nothing in there.
And I simply-- I have to have it.
They let me take it, that thing, along.
So I took this along with me, and I had it in the army.
There will be a story following after what happened to it.
But nevertheless, that's what we did.
I put this thing on every day.
Every day, unless there was a reason for it not to.
But otherwise, I put this thing on every day.
We didn't have long prayers for two hours or an hour and a half or something like that.
You said the blessing, whatever it is, and that was the end of it.
And of course-- and that's it.
So when we came across, you know, graves like that, I was saying Kaddish by heart because of my father passing away at 37.
I knew it by heart.
And didn't even, didn't need the book.
And the other Jewish boys who were older than I am, they knew also.
And whoever didn't have a book, they were sharing.
So we were doing this, and the Christian guys were kneeling and doing their prayers, crossing each other was fine.
We had a couple of Ukrainian guys there.
And they did their prayers, and that's was fine.
It was no, no disruption in that.
And that's the only thing what we did.
We came on a grave-- prayers, that's the first thing what we did.
It didn't help, but it helps here.
And that's the only thing-- helps you in your heart.
And that's it.
They definitely documented.
And of course, you know, we had this officers, high officers, there when they came.
Later, they tried to-- they started to lecture.
They still didn't mention anything about concentration camps.
There was no mention on the concentration camp until we entered them.
But they were talking.
Later, they started already to say, don't be surprised.
Going to be more here and more there and more here because they were.
So they let us know then.
But up to that point, there was no discussion about murdering or something like that.
We came very shortly because we entered Poland in July, in July, middle of July.
And the first concentration camp which we entered, just by pure luck, and by, by the people which they showed us what is going on there was Sobibor, and that was on the 22nd of July.
The 22nd of July, the first camp was Sobibor.
Now, it was a concentration camp, not a concentration but extermination camp.
Unfortunately, to our surprise, there was no building standing there.
As we found out later, of course, that there was a revolt in that camp.
And after the revolt, murders occurred.
In other words, the inmates murdered 10 or 11 people of the German people and some Ukrainian people.
And escape follow.
And that's-- I'm telling you the fact after which I read here.
What we saw-- the only sign what we saw is the railroad tracks leading to the, to the forest.
There was a sign 'Sobibor' in it.
And there was nothing there except planted-- there were, there were a hole sort of like a square reduced.
In order-- that was in the middle of the forest.
That forest was cleared completely from tall trees.
OK?

On this spot, where they had-- later we found out that that was the mass graves, what they did is, they planted-- we were told body Polish people there, body inhabitants, body farmers, that that was the extermination camp, people are buried in here, and the trees are planted.
The German planted the trees in in 1943, they started to plant the trees.
After dismantling the whole thing in 1943, they started to plant little trees to masquerade.
That's what we was told.
They pointed to us.
Oh, yes.
They knew everything what was going on.
They told us what-- they did their daily lives there.
Farmers, they did their daily lives, yeah.
And they told us about the screams and about the trains and about the whole thing.
That was the first time what we ever found out that there were such thing like crematoriums, gas chambers, and everything.
That was the first time what we found out right there in Sobibor.
From the Polish people.
No, they didn't prepare us on anything.
There was nothing discussed before that.
That was a village, which you were going in the direction of Lublin, and happens that part of the, of the division went through that.
There was not big resistance.
There was not-- after we crossed the borders, they were not big-- the bigger resistance started to be in Lublin.
They were fighting.
You'd carry your gun like that.
And that was it.
But there were no, no shooting or something like that.
That farm was not destroyed.
Let's put it this way.
The only destruction was with the people, and the camp was dismantled.
But there was no destruction as such.
Of course, you have to understand we were shooting on that town.
It's automatic.
It is automatic.
You don't try to shoot on the trees, I mean, on the houses.
You try to shoot on the farmlands because you know that the Germans were not sitting in the houses.
They would be, they would be, you know, in the-- what do you call it?
God, I forgot the name.
In the, in the little hole.
Yeah, the dugout, the trenches.
The trenches, that's right.
You see, I forgot the name.
And they would be in the trenches, so that's where we were shooting.
We were shooting special artillery.
Artillery-- we were preparing.
Artillery was always softening up the position.
Before the infantry moved anyplace, there was artillery barrage in every little town.
That's what it was.
But that's it.
As far as-- I didn't see one shell dropping on that, on that grave there, on that area there.
None of them dropped.
So they were shooting, but they were shooting advance shooting.
We did not have any resistance.
We did not have any resistance.
We did, did-- biggest resistance we got, which we got is when we entered, we were entering Lublin.
That was the biggest destruction what I saw.
On that site.
Of course we had resistance crossing the river.
That was big resistance.
Chelmo was on fire.
That was big resistance.
Wlodawa partially was bombarded.
But that particular town, Sobibor which was, it was not a town.
It was a village.
You've got a couple of houses.
You got the railroad station.
And the railroad station was the only-- it was-- I don't know if it was built by the Germans or not.
I really don't know.
But there was a forest tree there.
And they were taking the forest down and probably were taking the trees.
We would have never known.
No idea.
So, it was so disguised that you had no idea that it was a mass grave there.
And it was a big mass grave because, all right, there were crematoriums there and everything.
But there's, I don't know, about 250,000 people died in this camp.
Well, again, it was not a big shock yet because we didn't see any buildings.
Again, we saw a big mass grave masquerading with these little trees.
We still didn't know what an extermination camp means.
I mean, the guy said, oh, they were burning them.
They were burning the Jews.
And we saw flames coming out from the chimney.
But until you see that, that instrument, you just don't visualize anything.
So we still didn't know.
We just believed what they said.
Yeah, of course.
We believed them.
They were pretty descriptive because they were living right next to it.
They want to talk to us.
They want to talk to us.
And the, the strange thing is they probably are still living there.
They're probably still living there.
We encounter fighting.
We encounter fighting before Lublin.
Just about three, four kilometers before Lublin, we encounter fighting because we took the town really by surprise.
And all that mass destruction with artillery and with the bombardment from the air crafts really made a mess on the streets.
So the army, which was retreating, had a very hard time to pass through.
So they got caught in a, in a [? net. ?] But before we entered Lublin, we entered a real extermination camp.
And this is the first sign and the first structure which we ever saw was extermination camp Majdanek.
And that was on the 23rd of July.
One day later was the Majdanek.
And, of course, the same thing, we enter very, very carefully.
We opened the doors.
There was a gate.
A big gate, yes.
A big gate with, with, you know, wires and everything.
Barbed wires all around.
And there were buildings.
And we thought that-- we still didn't know it was a extermination camp.
Nobody told us that.
We thought it was barracks, military barracks because--
Yeah, exactly.
And as a matter of fact, we did.
We caught that four SS people and two Polish collaborators.
And I didn't do it myself, but our units did it.
And they were tried after the war.
But we still did not visualize.
We thought a factory, you know, a big factory.
Of course, we saw a chimney near the [INAUDIBLE].
We saw a tremendous amount of ashes.
But we still didn't know.
Maybe that's a factory.
Maybe it's something.
Maybe that's industrial waste, whatever it is.
We did know it was human, human ash until later, till about a half an hour later.
So that was Majdanek.
Now, if I give you the exact description of what I saw, I have it written up.
If you would like to hear that because I might miss something.
So what--
We saw buildings.
We notice, we notice that the houses were equally painted with light green, with light green.
We notice a chimney far away, a little bit away, not exactly by the door.
We notice big magazines of clothing.
Still there, full of clothing.
We noticed the big storage, open storage.
Now, it was like, it was like three walls, OK?
And then there was sort of like, like a screen, like a wire screen to enter the whole thing.
It was a big-- to this day, I remember, it was a tall, big tank.
And to enter, I don't know if it was a small door or not.
But it was sort of like a wired, a wire gate through the whole, through the whole thing with shoes.
And they had sorted out shoes-- ladies', men's, and children's separately, complete separately.
There were thousands of the shoes.
And everybody-- every shoe was sorted out, you know, by pair, it was sorted out.
And just there, one shelf after another.
So that's what we saw in that thing.
And there was ladies' and men's shoes.
And on the side, again, we saw, you know, a big mountain of ashes, which we did not know what it was.
And again, the magazines, you know, with the clothing.
And finally, we entered a crematorium, which at that time, we did not know a crematorium.
And the crematorium-- I know there was nothing there.
There was nothing there.
There was-- the crematorium was made from, from cement, cement blocks, exactly, I don't know the measure, but about 20 feet, about 20 feet by something, I don't remember exactly what.
And they had sort of like shower heads on the ceiling.
We didn't know what-- we thought it was water for shower.
They had very small opening in the steel doors that you can see from the outside.
You couldn't see anything from the inside.
But you could see from the outside.
They had small little openings, small windows, sort of with gates on the top of the ceiling, on the side of the ceiling.
And there was also, looking in from the-- on the top, was also sort of like an opening.
And that was the, that was the gas chambers.
They also, what I-- as far as I remember, they had benches around the edges.
I don't know how many benches.
I don't know exactly how many of these rooms I did see.
Then after that--
It was absolutely more than one.
More than one.
More than three.
Yes.
Absolutely.
Then, after that, somebody told us what it is.
One of the officers came in from the outside.
He already knew.
Found out, and he told us what it is.
Well, he told us, that's where people are gassed.
Simple as that.
A little farther, a little farther, there was a big stone building also.
And that's where the crematorium was there.
Now, as far as I remember, don't quote me, as far as I remember, there were six or seven of the crematoriums there.
I don't know exactly how many, six or seven.

And the way we found out that this ash is actually human ash because there was still some particles-- that crematorium, by the way, was working to the last day until the German soldiers departed from it.
We didn't find one single soul as far as the inmates.
By the end, by the-- about half a year before the war ended, there were 5,000 of Jewish soldiers living in camps, in POW camps, Jewish soldiers, which were interned after the 1939 war.
And they were living in the Lublin area there.
And in the last half a year, when they started to liquidate the Warsaw ghetto, they brought in the soldiers also.
We have found out that, of course, later. 5,000 Jewish POW were burned in the camp, in Majdanek.
And anyway, so we saw still unfinished bodies sitting in the ovens, bones which didn't burn through.
After close inspection for the ashes later, we also found a lot of bones which didn't burn through, together with the stink you had there.
And that was it.
And we were explained what people were doing there.
The remains in it.
I don't remember really.
I don't remember, but they could have burnt quite a few bodies.
That's was a pretty big opening.
Oh, yeah.
That's what could have been-- pretty big openings.
How many they were burning, I don't know.
Polish people.
They were not specifying Jews and not Jews.
Polish people.
The deeper we-- when we found out it was mostly Jews, when we took over that city Lublin.
Not so much Lublin.
Like [NON-ENGLISH] Not Prague, Czechoslovakia.
[NON-ENGLISH] There I spoke with the people.
There I spoke with the people.
And of course, later, while the, while the war was progressing, the officers already prepared us.
They were political officers which didn't have nothing to do with a gun, just the mentality to let you know what's, what's to expect.
They told us that the majority in those camps would be just so, like Sobibor, 100%.
Not 100% because they had some POW, Russian POW also in Sobibor.
But 90% of the people in that camp.
And the same goes for the, for the Majdanek. 90% of the people were Jewish people.
And they were from the area.
There, and from the area all over Europe.
They didn't specify from what city.
We didn't stay long.
In all those camps, I want you to know, all those camps which we liberated, now Sobibor, where we didn't stay longer than 45 minutes. 45 minutes.
Majdanek, we stayed close to two hours.
Yes, to look around.
To look around.
We saw, yes.
All the, all the barracks were left.
All the barracks were left.
They-- I know they were all painted neatly green.
The area was nice and clean, kept very nice.
The barbed wire and they had all those watch towers which they have, everything was erect, and nothing was taken down.

The only thing which was destroyed, later we found out and we were also told by the political people there, that it was their building from their, from their commander of that camp, a German commander.
I don't know his name.
I don't remember the name.
But a, a German commander was living in that, that building was partially destroyed.
That's the only-- it was a wooden building, like one-family house.
It was right next to the crematorium.
Didn't bother him.
It was right there.
And there were also gardens which we saw.
There were gardens there, which they, they planted, the SS people which were living there.
Nearby, they had big gardens.
There, later, we found out the ash was used for the gardens too.
Very badly.
Do you know, you know, this is the first time-- I'm not a crier.
I was sent away, you know, from my parents, and I was still a kid, really, 16 years old.
And in, in, in Poland, you were a kid.
Today, you're a 16-year-old, you're a little bit more mature.
But they were, you know, you were sheltered by your mother, by your grandfather, by everybody.
So you were still a kid.
This is the first time I cried.
I really did.
And everyone, the whole group of people which I was with, were crying.
And of course, again, I don't want to repeat it too often, but again we said Kaddish, and the Gentile people prayed.
But no, we didn't take it too good.
And yet, we had to recuperate.
We have to go on fighting.
Well, not very nice.
Not very nice.
Very ferocious, but strict orders, whoever falls in your hand, don't you dare kill him.
Strict orders.
But nevertheless, people still killed them.
But that was strict orders not to, not to--
Whoever we captured goes to captivity.
We captured those four SS people and to collaborated, they went to captivity.
Each soldier which we captured was not our business to shoot them.
It was against-- you can be, yeah, you can be prosecuted for that.
It was a strict law, seriously.
Because the reason why they did, I don't know if they did it for humane reason, I think they mostly did it because they didn't want the German army to know that the army is shooting people.
This way they would resist harder not to give in.
They were hoping if you will not shoot them, they will give up.
Surrender.
I don't know, it's to deter.
It was fine.
But I don't know if it worked.
But the fact is we, we couldn't shoot.
We were not allowed to shoot.
Yes, there were, there were private instances.
The other guy was trying to run away so you shot him.
He didn't run away, but you shot him anyway.
That's the way it was done.
I, myself, be honest with you, I was from, from, from 1943 to 1945 on the front lines, I never took a gun-- I had two guns on me and one automatic.
I never shoot anybody in the back.
Never did it.
Of course, my cannon killed many, many people.
No question about it.
But I didn't see their eyes.
I simply wouldn't do it.
I was not chicken.
One on one.
If I would come on one on one, the way it was in Berlin, no question about it.
But I still was carrying-- there, you know, I still was shooting with a gun, with a cannon.
But if somebody would come from behind, something like that, of course, if I had a chance, if he didn't get me, I had to get him.
But the fact is just when I could have-- it might come up later.
I don't know.
I caught 10 SS people.
Myself, 10 SS people and one captain.
I got help later, but I caught, caught 10 SS people.
And how did I caught them?
And that was in [NON-ENGLISH] That will come up later.
I don't want to discuss it now.
Well, we are, so let's hold it.
And we'll get to it when we get there.
But I did not shoot them.
I just interrogate them.
And they got to that good place, and I shoot them.
Don't worry about it.
Straight to the front line.
Straight to the front line, full speed to Lublin.
Lublin was only two kilometers away.
I mean, as I say, we were at there two hours.
That was a lot.
And you know, sometimes a minute, you think that's an hour.
And sometimes an hour thinks that's a minute.
So I don't know correctly if it was two hours or not.
But, short time.
We went through the facility, said the prayers, and out you go.
Out you go.
And the fact is, not only that, we taught ourselves we saw what we did.
But we also want to stay alive, so we have to concentrate what we see to remember what we saw.
Seriously, that was, that was our mind.
Myself anyway.
I have to concentrate and see what I saw and not lose my, my mind because there's still a long time to go home.
So that's exactly what we did.
We left like would have if nothing happened.
There was deep down in here.
But in your mind, nothing happened.
You just stay alive.
That's all.
That's exactly what everybody else did.
Otherwise, it wouldn't work.
I wouldn't be here today.
So then it was a big battle to take Lublin?
It was a big battle to take Lublin.
Yes, it was a tremendous battle for one reason because the amount of soldiers which were trapped really there.
And they fought.
I never saw so many, so much destruction.
That's the first time, really, which I saw destruction of human beings and animals.
When I say animals, I mean horses.
Please.
Thank you.
